behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is not a module handoff bug — it is whether the real LLM uses the email context. LLM-DEP: A mocked LLM trivially returns a pre-crafted contextual reply, hiding the failure entirely. MOCK-HIDE: Any deterministic stub passes by construction. Only a real LLM call can reveal whether the model actually grounds its reply in the thread content.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real email thread containing specific named entities, dates, and topics to the LLM-based reply generator. Assert that the returned draft contains at least N of those specific items (e.g., names, figures, dates) and does not consist solely of generic filler phrases unrelated to the thread.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Factual consistency checking in this pipeline is semantically driven — it must understand that a claim contradicts a KB entry, not just that a keyword differs. A deterministic stub would always flag or never flag, masking real failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the checker returns a pre-set flag regardless of actual claim–KB relationship. The real failure (model fails to detect semantic contradiction) only surfaces with a live model call.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the knowledge base with specific factual entries (e.g., 'Product X was released in 2022'). Submit a draft containing a direct contradiction ('Product X was released in 2019') to the consistency checker backed by a real model. Assert the checker returns a flag or warning identifying the contradicting claim, and does not flag a non-contradicting claim about the same product.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The failure mode is that the real LLM ignores the 'no KB results' signal and asserts fabricated specifics anyway. Integration-level testing with a mocked LLM trivially passes by returning a pre-written disclaimer. MOCK-HIDE: Only a real LLM call reveals whether the model respects the empty-KB condition or hallucinates instead.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the knowledge base retrieval to return zero results for the query topic. Submit a domain-specific email thread to the real LLM-based draft generator. Assert the output contains a disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'I'm not certain', 'please verify') and does not assert specific factual claims (dates, figures, named entities) that were absent from both the email thread and the KB.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is a control-flow bug at the module boundary between the approval state machine and the send module — a real module-handoff failure, not a single-function logic bug. LLM-DEP: LLM output quality is irrelevant; the test is about whether send() is ever called before approval is recorded. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the send function and asserting it is not called is appropriate here — the dependency being mocked (email transport) is not what is under test. No LLM or OS/network behavior is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the approval workflow with a pre-generated draft (no LLM needed). Assert that the send function (mocked via patch.object) is not called when the workflow is advanced without an approval event. Then submit an explicit approval action and assert send is called exactly once with the draft content.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names multiple agentic steps in sequence: thread ingestion → LLM draft generation → LLM/model-based fact check → approval gate. Each step with a real model can fail independently, and inter-step state handoff (draft content, KB flags, approval status) can be lost between steps. No single step or module pair captures this; only a full pipeline run does.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real email thread into the full pipeline. Observe in order: thread is parsed and passed to the LLM; the LLM returns a draft; the draft is passed to the fact checker with the real KB; any flagged claims are surfaced; the pipeline halts at the approval gate. Assert that (a) the draft references the thread, (b) KB contradictions are flagged if seeded, and (c) no send occurs until an explicit approval is injected into the pipeline.
